items.q: What is reduction in terms of electrons?
items.correct: Gain of electrons (the oxidation number is reduced).
items.misconception: Reduction means losing oxygen and oxidation means gaining oxygen — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Reduction means a substance becomes physically smaller in size.
items.why: Students learn the oxygen definition first (from rusting/combustion) and never update to the more general electron-transfer definition.

items.q: At which electrode are positive ions reduced during electrolysis?
items.correct: At the cathode (the negative electrode) — positive ions gain electrons there.
items.misconception: Positive ions are reduced at the anode because that's where positive things go.
items.random_wrong: Positive ions are oxidised at the cathode by losing protons.
items.why: Students associate 'positive' with 'attracted to positive' or get muddled by the sign conventions and switch the electrodes.

items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: Which is the correct half-equation at the cathode for molten lead bromide?
items.why: It's Pb²⁺ + 2e⁻ → Pb. “Pb → Pb²⁺ + 2e⁻” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Why does the anode in aluminium electrolysis need replacing regularly?
items.correct: Oxygen made there reacts with hot carbon to form CO₂
items.why: Correct answer: Oxygen made there reacts with hot carbon to form CO₂. “It dissolves in the cryolite” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: A copper-rich plant ash contains 8% copper by mass. How much copper (in kg) is in 250 kg of ash?
items.why: Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
